title: $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational — pick a tactic
hint: fork: try direct vs. by contradiction · ←/→ to scrub · click any visited node

nodes.prompt: Claim. $\sqrt{2} \notin \mathbb{Q}$ — there is no pair of integers $p, q$ with $q \ne 0$ such that $\sqrt{2} = p/q$.
Two natural tactics. Direct: try to construct an explicit non-rational form for $\sqrt{2}$. By contradiction: assume $\sqrt{2} = p/q$ in lowest terms and derive a contradiction. Pick one.

Direct: write $\sqrt{2} = a + b$ for some $a \in \mathbb{Q}$ and a non-rational remainder $b$. But to show $b$ is non-rational we'd need a separate proof of irrationality — the technique just punts the problem.
Stuck. Direct construction begs the question: any candidate decomposition reduces to proving some other number irrational. This branch teaches us the right move is contradiction.
Suppose, for contradiction, $\sqrt{2} = p/q$ with $\gcd(p, q) = 1$ — i.e. the fraction is in lowest terms.
Squaring: $2 = p^2 / q^2$, so $p^2 = 2 q^2$. Hence $p^2$ is even, which forces $p$ to be even (the parity of $p^2$ matches the parity of $p$).
Write $p = 2k$ for some integer $k$. Substitute back: $(2k)^2 = 2 q^2 \Rightarrow 4 k^2 = 2 q^2 \Rightarrow q^2 = 2 k^2$. So $q^2$ is even, and therefore $q$ is even.
We now know $2 \mid p$ and $2 \mid q$. Either we trust $\gcd(p, q) = 1$ from the assumption, or we don't. Pick the move that produces the contradiction.
But the assumption was $\gcd(p, q) = 1$, i.e. $p$ and $q$ share no common factor — yet we just showed $2$ divides both. Contradiction.

If we drop the coprimality assumption we can keep dividing $p$ and $q$ by $2$ forever, generating an infinite descending chain of positive integers — itself a contradiction (well-ordering of $\mathbb{N}$). Slightly longer route, same destination.
QED. The assumption $\sqrt{2} = p/q$ in lowest terms led to $2 \mid \gcd(p, q)$, contradicting $\gcd(p, q) = 1$. So no such pair $(p, q)$ exists, and $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational. $\blacksquare$

QED. Iterating $p \mapsto p/2$, $q \mapsto q/2$ produces an infinite decreasing sequence of positive integers, contradicting the well-ordering of $\mathbb{N}$. So $\sqrt{2} \notin \mathbb{Q}$. $\blacksquare$
